17.1
Introduction
Rapid developments in technology have enabled humanity to manipu-
late their environment, making changes according to human interests and
needs. While humankind has played a significant role in changing the Earth
through history, this role was likely relatively harmless until industrializa-
tion. Afterward, long-lasting, inexpensive, and rapid production to provide
for the growing human population and their needs became the focus. The
resulting environmental problems continued to accumulate throughout the
Earth, including chemicals affecting the microbial and pesticidal chains,
toxic chemicals, non-controlled gasses poisoning the air, land, water, and
slow decomposing packaging. The results of human interventions through
rapid deforestation, exploitation of natural sources, and massive land use of
human has apparently settlements exceeded nature’s self-repair capacity. 489
Biomimicry for Materials, Design and Habitats. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-821053-6.00004-7
© 2022 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved. Today, civilization is steadily exploiting natural resources to maintain an
ever-increasing rate of growth. While the cities grow larger and buildings tal-
ler, the rate of deforestation has increased. One way to address this urgent
issue can be by a better understanding of the negative environmental impact
of humans, and constructing new buildings with higher efficiency and
enhanced standards. We are beginning to realize our significant dependence
on ecosystems [1]. While all organisms are part of a “biome” that is part of
the “biosphere,” every organism’s continued prosperity is dependent on the
health of the “biosphere.” Under these circumstances, the biosphere cycle
could degrade, reducing the sustainability life as we kn

ow it on Earth. From
this perspective, the construction industry is one of the main drivers of the
disturbance of the biosphere’s balance, with manifold potential harm to the
environment. Approximately 40% of global energy use and 50% of global raw material
consumption by weight occurs within the construction industry [2]. Shaping
raw materials into the desired construction materials requires massive energy
use with resulting emissions into the air, water, and soil. These materials gen-
erally tend to be heavy, causing a considerable emission(s) during their trans-
port. Also, a wide range of wastes and chemicals pollute the land and water
during the construction process while changing the land itself. Moreover, in
the usage phase, residential demands in heating and cooling cause a significant
amount of greenhouse gas emissions throughout the whole planet. Most build-
ing components become waste after the economic life of a building and also
their disposal requires energy, cause air pollution, water contamination, and
biodiversity losses. Very few building materials can be reused or recycled in
the same quality. Recycling usually ends up as down-cycling, meaning that
the material would not be recycled after another use. The non-biodegradability
and long lifespan of these processed building materials prevent them from
returning to nature [3]. Accordingly, the global construction industry is respon-
sible for around 40% of global greenhouse gas emissions in various phases,
manufacturing the raw materials, construction, usage, and demolishing
(Fig. 17.1). Therefore the building industry is facing unprecedented challenges
to reduce the environmental impact of current consumption [4]. However, this
also presents low-cost and high impact opportunities. Beginning with the second half of the 20th century, questioning of the
relationship of engineering and architecture, actually all design disciplines,
with nature has been an important concern. Kennedy et al. pointed out that a
traditionally designed building could be deficient in terms of how it fits
within the larger ecosystem [5]. Therefore the term sustainability has gained
importance on a global scale and has dominated academic research [13,5]. It has become a “mega-concept” that controls production practices through
agreements between countries and the development of certifications. Within this context, many architectural theories were developed over the
last century—from eco-architecture to bio-architecture. The goal behind such
490
PART | IV Biomimetic habitation: Architecture and sustainable environments

ideologies is to create a building that respects the environment or at least has
a significantly less environmental impact than conventional practices. The
design of the built environment is becoming gradually more important to
maintain the quality of life and biodiversity. This situation led the designers
to answer the question of “is it possible to design buildings, which have posi-
tive effe

cts on the environment?” If inspiration for architectural designs
comes from life, buildings can be considered and designed as parts of the liv-
ing system. Therefore it is possible to design buildings with the potential to
produce energy, materials, clean air, and water while creating a circular rela-
tionship between the inorganic and organic to create a complex, adaptive,
and cyclic system that acts in the same way as the ecosystem [1]. Professionals in architecture and the engineering disciplines are constantly
searching for solutions to many problems related to being environmentally
responsible. This perspective has led to (1) the increasing importance given
to nature-based knowledge and nature-inspired/biomimetic approaches and
(2) intensifying interdisciplinary relationships, especially engineering, design,
and science. Alongside many disciplines, integration of architecture and biol-
ogy has great potential since biotechnology and biology’s developments lead
to understanding how nature works. In this direction, interdisciplinary studies,
FIGURE 17.1
Greenhouse gas emissions in different phases of the construction industry. Source: O. Kırdo¨k and Biodesign Team Turkey. Mycelium biocomposites in architecture Chapter | 17
491

which are inspired by nature and related to the fields of science and biology/
biotechnology, have a principal place in today’s design strategies. A new
approach that includes living organisms into the design that goes beyond inspi-
ration from nature is “biodesign via bio-collaboration.”
This idea seeks an architecture belonging to nature by integrating living
organisms either to the design process or to the final product through bio-
collaborative relationships, such as in the examples of protocells [6],
calcifying bacteria [7], silkworms [8], and fungi [9]. Bio-collaborative design
processes produce complicated living and non-living biological products
from raw materials like living cells or molecules. Adamatzky et al. [4] stated
that the “innovative architectures co-exist/co-evolve with living substrates”. In bio-collaborative design, bio-based materials and composites are an
issue of interest in the quest over replacing the late centuries’ unsustainable
practices and materials used in the industries. This new approach has benefi-
cial potential and would probably lead to a significant change in the design
and construction industry’s future. In this context, this study focuses on dis-
cussing the possibilities of mycelium in the design and construction sector to
create a bio-based material. 17.2
Mycelium as a biodesign collaborator
Carbon is a crucial component of all life on Earth. The “carbon cycle”
within the biosphere means “life cycle” and is based on the ongoing life on
Earth [10]. The soil’s decay cycle is also an essential process in the carbon
cycle (Fig. 17.2). In this cycle, nearly all plants in natural ecosystems have a
symbiotic relationship with fungi. Fungi are a kingdom of organisms, one of
the olde

st inhabitants of the Earth, with a primal role in natural ecosystems
as decomposers [11]. Mycelial fungi play an essential role in the decomposi-
tion of dead plant material. This is vital in putting nutrients back into the
soil, helping the plants grow [12]. Fungi are essential components of nature’s digesting system, making them
promising components when designing biodegradable and bio-collaborative
materials. They have been an important part of human existence for millennia
and were used as food sources, as medicine, and for spiritual experience. Today, they are used in the food and beverage industry, such as bread making
and alcoholic beverage production, as well as being a direct food source, such
as mushrooms. Besides, some metabolites they produce are used as drugs in
medicine, and their enzymes act as catalysts in industrial processes. They can
also be used to produce cruelty-free leather, building blocks, furniture, and
many other products. Utilization of this easy to grow bio-based material for
creating a sustainable circular economy can be possible by scaling up with
mass production techniques [13]. 492
PART | IV Biomimetic habitation: Architecture and sustainable environments

The architecture of today uses many different materials to realize desired
design expectations. However, many of these materials cannot be sustained over
their production cycle since they are not biodegradable and consume more
resources than the Earth can replenish. Therefore cost-competitive, healthy and
sustainable alternatives are necessary. Cast on-site or precast installations from
mycelium-based biocomposites show high potential to replace many surfaces in
buildings from inner walls to building panels. They are also lightweight, which
is a desired property, and expected to reduce dead loads on the building struc-
ture. Mycelium acts as a binding agent, which leads to many desired properties
in thus bound biocomposites, ranging from heat insulation to stiffness. The rapid
growth and other potential properties of mycelium-based bio-composite materi-
als make them an interesting research topic. As they can be cast to achieve non-
allergic supportive environments for human health, mycelium-based composites
are a good alternative to today’s choice of materials ranging in many industries
from building to fashion. Therefore many researchers today try to understand
the physical limits, properties, and potentials of mycelium-based biocomposite
products in their works. However, most of these applications are still at the
experimental stage and require more research before becoming common ele-
ments in the architectural design or construction sector [14]. FIGURE 17.2
Recycle/upcycle in nature. Source: O. Kırdo¨k and Biodesign Team Turkey. Mycelium biocomposites in architecture Chapter | 17

17.2.1
Biological background of mycelial fungi
Although most fungal life cycles are hidden underground in soil, including
consuming organic matter, the most familiar vision o

f the fungi that comes to
the mind is their fruiting bodies; mushrooms, molds, or yeast. Accordingly,
the term mushroom is commonly used to identify the edible sporophores,
and the umbrella-shaped fruiting body (sporophore) of a specific type of fungi
[15]; however, that is only the reproductive part of the life cycle of fungi. As these mushrooms sprout above the ground, they form specialized cells on
the gills or the pores to produce spores to be released into the air. Besides
reproduction, spores are the way of mushroom-producing fungi to widen their
region by moving to different places. Any ground that supports suitable
growth conditions that a spore lands on happens to be a base for the coloniza-
tion of new fungal tissue. Generally called “substrate,” this base becomes the food, moisture, and
heat source while acting as a scaffold for fungus to grow. The substrate can
be soil or tree bark, as long as it contains the resources that a fungus needs
to grow. If the environmental conditions are within the necessary range,
spores will begin to germinate and form the mycelial structure. When a spore
sprouts, it starts to form hyphae; 210-µm-wide microscopic cellular fiber,
generally based on chitin or cellulose, or in some situations both. This is the
primary unit of the fungi that forms its biomass. Enzymes secreted by these
fibers break down the polymers in the substrate and provide the nutrients
required for growth. These fibers multiply and form three-dimensional net-
works over the substrate in time as they decompose the complex molecular
structures they are attached to. These hyphae networks keep multiplying
through the food source and become the fungal tissue called a mycelium. As
a result, fungal biomass replaces some organic matter within the substrate
particles and forms bonding structures with the substrate [14]. Depending on food source availability, mushroom mycelia can live up to
hundreds of years or die in a few months. When the oldest parts cannot reach
nutrients, these cells begin to die and become a base of growth for bacteria. These bacteria digest the mushroom to release the nutrients back to feed the
soil, while spores released from fruiting bodies of mushroom form new
hyphal networks [16]. As long as there are enough nourishment and
suitable environmental conditions, the mycelium will form new spawns of
mushrooms to sustain its existence and growth [15]. As a result, the mycelial
structure can enlarge through the underground in time via resource veins to
form massive biomasses. An estimated 80,000 to 120,000 species have been documented in the
fungi kingdom; there are thought to be about 1.5 million species [17]. The
environmental conditions for growth vary depending on the fungus type. There are many studies on how different types of fungi grow on specific sub-
strates [13,1723]. In nature, fungi preferably digest lignin and cellulose,
494
PART | IV Biomimetic habitation: Architecture and sustainable environments

the prima

ry structural components of wood [24]. On the other hand, their
ability to decompose complex molecules makes them an excellent tool for
the environmental bioremediation of agricultural waste and reclamation of
pollutant chemical compounds, such as textile dyes [25], therapeutics [26],
explosive residues, and many others in the soil [14]. Growth temperatures and moisture balance are essential factors for the
formation of mycelium-based composites. These composites comprise bio-
logically grown filamentous hyphae networks rather than expensive energy-
intensive manufacturing processes to convert low-cost organic wastes into
economically viable and environmentally friendly materials. White-rot fungi
of the Pleurotus and Ganoderma genera are the most-often used species hav-
ing strong mycelium fibers capable of growing in lignocellulosic biomass,
agricultural by-products, or waste materials for the applications discussed in
this chapter. Until now, the ability to grow using a wide range of substrates
has been singled out as the principal feature of these fungi, linked to their
many and notorious production of ligninolytic enzymes. Substrates affect the mechanical properties of mycelium-based biocompo-
sites. Haneef et al. [27] tested two fungal species, Ganoderma lucidum and
Pleurotus ostreatus, grown on two substrate types. One with potato dextrose-
added microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) and the other with just MCC alone,
which is harder to digest as a fungal substrate. MCC substrate brings out a
stiffer composite. Elsacker et al. [23] analyzed mechanical, physical, and
chemical characteristics of Trametes versicolor on five different substrates
(hemp, flax, flax waste, softwood, straw) and fiber conditions (loose,
chopped, dust, pre-compressed, and tow). Their test results show that the
final product’s mechanical performance depends more on the fiber condition,
size, and processing than on the chemical composition of the fibers. It is possible to achieve different results through genetic modification of
a fungus. An exemplary study of the mycelium of a Schizophyllum commune
strain with hydrophobin gene sc3 inactivated shows 34 times more maxi-
mum tensile strength than non-modified examples [28]. This genetically
modified strain retains more water when compared to the wild type strain. This is explained by the fact that the encoded protein coats aerial hyphae
with a hydrophobic coating. 17.2.2
Studies on biodegradable mycelium composites
Mycelium-based materials are a novel research topic with broad interest due
to numerous practical applications. Many studies contain different trials to
constitute biocomposite materials [2932]. Mycelium-based composites
have been developed into a wide range of products such as packaging, con-
struction and insulation materials, leather-like textile materials, and transpar-
ent renewable films [13]. Mycelium is a highly versatile and fast-growing
renewable resource, entirely natural, and can grow on any given sha

pe; some
Mycelium biocomposites in architecture Chapter | 17
495

species last very long and offer good fire resistance [33]. Mycelium-based
composites with different structural or visual properties can be produced by
changing the fungi type and substrate; this allows the composite to be used
in different functions. Girometta et al. [34] provide a comprehensive review of mycelium-based
biocomposites’ physical, mechanical, and thermal properties and the factors
affecting these properties. Islam et al. [21] point out that the network density,
dimensions, elasticity, branching, and entanglement influence the mycelium
composite products’ mechanical aspects. Appels et al. [29] studied the fac-
tors that may affect the final morphology of the product and found out: the
type of substrate, the species of fungus, and post-processing methods, such
as hot pressing, can influence the morphology, density, tensile and flexural
strength, and water absorption properties. In many studies reported in this chapter, mycelial growth occurs in
molds. The fungus is inoculated into lignocellulosic substrates for feeding
and support—fungal biomass grows inside the mold by eating a part of the
substrate and binding the remaining parts. The resulting filamentous materi-
als have low density and are highly anisotropic. Many researchers employ a
similar production methodology, which begins with molding the substrate
into desired forms that would originate a base to feed the inoculated fungi
and to generate mycelium networks. According to the design and material
expectations, forms are generated by either growing monolithically or assem-
bling units. The majority of the studies continue their process by killing the
living fungal tissue by heating or freezing. The dead mycelium solidifies its
connections and acts as a binder that holds the substrate particles. All these
processes require particular sterile work and environmental conditions to
form a healthy mycelium network. Substrate selection and composition rates vary depending on the type of
product desired. Different substrate combinations significantly affect the
mechanical performance, strength, and hardness of the mycelium-based bio-
composite [9]. Also, fiber type, particle size, and processing of the substrate,
depending on the chemical composition, affect the material’s strength [9,35]. To keep the cost of mycelium composites low and facilitate the recycling of
wastes, low-cost agricultural plant-based [9] or forestry by-products or
wastes are used as fibrous substrates (such as straw) or particulate substrates
such as sawdust [36]. Lelivelt et al. [35] stated that in different substrates
(hemp varieties and wood) non-woven hemp is compatible with mushrooms
and provides high-pressure resistance. Ghazvinian et al. [37] aimed to
increase the mechanical and chemical properties of mycelium-based compos-
ite blocks in wall construction, and the results of compressive strength tests
on block samples showed that straw-base

d biocomposites could not bear
loads while chip-based biocomposites could be used in place of conventional
stacking units when sufficient reinforcement was used. Meanwhile, Appels
et al. [28] pointed out the potentials of modification growth conditions and
496
PART | IV Biomimetic habitation: Architecture and sustainable environments

applying gene modifications like deletion of hydrophobin gene, which would
result from an increase in density and branching of the fungal hypha to con-
trol mycelium composite to show desired properties. Some studies examine the strength and durability of mycelium-based bio-
material compared with plastic-based foams [14,17,34]. Attias et al. [14]
pointed out that mycelium-based foams show lower density, flexural
strength, and water absorbance when compared with EPS foams. Hence, the
development of mycelium composites that compete with synthetic alterna-
tives is progressing. Reducing dried material density, increasing flexural
strength, and decreasing water permeability is necessary for commercial
applications. Jones et al. ran a series of experiments regarding mycelium-
based foams’ fire-retardant properties. They used rice husk, agricultural
waste, and natural fire retardant, which contain high lignin and phosphorus
contents that are favored by the fungi, and reported promising results [19]. 17.2.3
Studies on using textiles in the mycelium-based composites
Various outstanding studies utilize fabric reinforcement in mycelium-based
composites
[31,3740]. While
the
biocomposites
mentioned
in
Section 17.2.2 are suitable for many applications, a mycelium sandwich
composite can be generated by adding another element—a fabric layer—to
the sides (and maybe inside) of the biocomposite (core). In this way, textiles
provide a fiber surface onto which the mycelium can easily hold on the skin
of a mycelium sandwich composite. This would keep the composite intact
against dispersion and impact in addition to increasing its tensile strength. The main advantages of mycelium-based composite materials made using
natural fibers (e.g., jute, flax, and cellulose fiber) over traditional synthetic
composites include lower cost, lower density, competitive strength, tensile
and impact mechanical properties, reduced energy consumption, the potential
for CO2 sequestration if done at a large scale, and perhaps most important of
all, tailorable biodegradability [31]. Jiang et al. pointed out that the bio-
based core and textile reinforcement can offer enhanced structural and ther-
mal performance since the mycelium would act as a binder like a polymer
resin matrix. Furthermore, better acoustic performance is a possible addi-
tional benefit of this application [38]. Elbasti and Alacam [40] used a growth kit from a local supplier containing
wheat grains and sawdust inoculated with P. ostreatus. Although the supplier
suggests 1221 days over the substrate for full mycelium growth, just 6 days
of monitored growth was enough to provide evidence of

the utility of this
approach since the purpose of the research was to observe the material’s
adaptability to the fabric formwork [40]. However, their test conditions were
less than ideal, as the mycelium mixture in the growing kit was not homoge-
neous; these kits tend to be easily contaminated in uncontrolled environmental
conditions. Therefore the results of this experiment can only be seen as a
Mycelium biocomposites in architecture Chapter | 17
497

success from an esthetic or proof-of-concept perspective. However, according
to Elbasti and Alacam, the first 2 days is the best choice to change the form-
work, while the ability to adapt falls drastically 5 days after inoculation [40]. Jiang et al. studied the production of mycelium-based sandwich biocom-
posites [30,31,38,39,41]. They used natural textile fabrics pre-glued with
bio-glue to create a laminar fabric layer to be used as a preformed shell for
the cultivated substrate. They tested infusing the preform with bio-resin to
terminate production and to improve its mechanical properties significantly. More mycelium-bound substrate areas were determined to be more robust,
but after the resin infusion strengthened the skin, the core sandwich seemed
to be the weakest point in the biocomposite, which showed deformation
under stress. 17.2.4
Patents and startups
Numerous patents on mycelium-enhanced materials and applications have
appeared during the last decade; their rate of publication has increased over
the past few years. All of them reveal that mycelium is a strong binder,
grown by inoculating cells on waste materials and bind them. They empha-
size that the binding process’s end product does not have as many harmful
environmental effects as many materials we use today that cause high carbon
emissions. The mycelium-based materials can be produced and decomposed
in the soil after their expected lifetime. Early patents focus on how mycelial
growth develops on lignin-based wastes or agar and are intended to recog-
nize the organism’s development process and the necessary environmental
conditions [4245]; among these, processes that report growth of fungi with-
out producing fruit are essential [43]. Many of the following-discussed patents concern the production of biode-
gradable composite material from mycelium and researching different spe-
cies of mushrooms, different types of wastes (e.g., hemp, wool, cotton,
sawdust, wood, coconut, sisal, cereal straw, rice straw, corn, barley, oats, sor-
ghum, kapok, flax, hemp, jute, ramie, cotton, stinging nettle, animal fibers,
cardboard): using different molds and production mechanisms [46], by a pro-
duction process by pressing in roll form [47], through production by weaving
the hyphae on a grid-frame [48], forming on a grid frame [49], or by mold-
ing for block production [50]. Some of these patents contain recommenda-
tions for using the composite material as a packaging material in small-scale
industrial products, or in the manufacture of therm

al/acoustic insulation
panels or fire-resistant panels, or some textile products. The resulting product
is resistant to external environmental conditions, a good insulator, and fire-
resistant [51,52]; it can also be produced quickly and economically. Nearly 100 patents deal with licensing rights in the United States and
China, and there are a small number of Korean and Canadian patents. Eleven
patents about mycelium-based composite materials are still valid; four of
498
PART | IV Biomimetic habitation: Architecture and sustainable environments

them encompass the entire world [5356]; and seven of them are only valid
in European countries [5763]. One of the pioneers of research and industrial application of mycelium-
based products is the firm Ecovative. In 2007 Ecovative started to commer-
cialize its mycelium-based products, such as chipboards wherein glue is
replaced by mycelium, insulation, or packaging mycelium foam and do-it-
yourself kit to grow mycelium. Their range of products includes packaging
products, which are being produced for different partner firms that use this
technology, such as Dell, Ikea, Biomason, Gunlocke, Bolt Threads, and
Sealed Air. Ecovative also makes its products using genetically modified
mushrooms according to its patent [43]. Thanks to the modification, fruit for-
mation is not observed in this fungus; thus it is convenient for designers. However, this mushroom has no production continuity since it does not pro-
duce spores due to the lack of fruiting bodies. Several other startup firms, like Fungalogic [64], focused on creating
building materials out of agricultural waste streams, using fungi to grow
waste into valuable resources. One of the current products of the company is
an acoustic panel made from mycelium and tomato stems. The company
offers furniture designs like phone booths made from these acoustic panels
for the work/office environment’s needs. 17.3
Potential use of mycelium in architectural design
and construction: Overview of in-house efforts
Over the last two decades, there have been several architectural exam-
ples designed and built with mycelium-based composites. Mycelium was
used as a material for building blocks or more extraordinary elements
[6567]. Building blocks can be produced by molding and using blocks,
similar to traditional masonry systems, suitable to the nature of the material. It is also frequently seen that computer-aided drawing programs and compu-
tational design methods support the design and production process [68]. Some current plans propose building with live fungal tissue to benefit from
its biological potential [4]. Some others intend to use mycelium base to grow
vegetation. A closed material cycle is a characteristic of the material made
of mushroom mycelium. Substrate and mycelium originate from nature and
can be returned to nature 100% at the end of the life cycle. In Fig. 17.3, the
closed life cycle of these material types when intended for architectural
application

s is illustrated. In Fig. 17.3, the following life stages are illustrated as follows:
1. A nutrient-rich substrate is inoculated with mycelium and incubation
began within sterile conditions. This helps the fungus to start creating the
roots responsible for the mechanical strength as the binder in the
Mycelium biocomposites in architecture Chapter | 17
499

resulting biocomposite before the colonized substrate is transferred to a
non-sterile, less nutrient-rich substrate. 2. According to the defined mold geometry, the colonized substrate is
placed together with non-sterile substrate together in a mold to start colo-
nization over a free substrate. 3. The fully colonized material is then removed from the mold and dried. 4. Transport to the end-user takes place. Another option could be growing
on the construction site to avoid transportation. 5. The product can serve for a specific life cycle for temporary structures or
extended periods if the surface is well treated and if appropriate dry serv-
ing conditions are applied. 6. At the end of the life cycle, the material can be 100% composted, taking
around three months. 7. The compost can be applied as fertilizer, and agricultural residues after
harvesting can be used back again as substrates to fabricate more
mycelium-based biocomposite building materials. In 2014, Ecovative produced mycelium bricks (Mycobricks) for the Hi-
Fy project by The Living [69], the largest architectural structure made of
mycelium so far, exhibited at the MoMA PS1 [70]. This project’s inspiration
comes from a design by mycologist Paul Stamets and artist Philip Ross in
2012 [71]. The pavilion had an internal metal structure covered with myce-
lium bricks. That same year Ross introduced Mycotectural Alpha, a small
structure made of live mycelium bricks that continuously colonized the sub-
strate throughout the exhibition [72]. The mycelium networks fused the
bricks without adhesive while also providing fresh mushroom harvests. FIGURE 17.3
Illustration of the mycelium-based building product closed life cycle. Source:
L. Strobel and BioMat at ITKE/University of Stuttgart. 500
PART | IV Biomimetic habitation: Architecture and sustainable environments

Another installation, “Mycotecture,” built from G. lucidium, a.k.a. Reishi
mushroom cultures, was grown in the shape of a brick to form an architec-
tural pavilion. It promotes this material’s potential while serving visitors tea
made from a piece of the fungus grown over the structure. Tea made from
reishi mushroom is known to have healing remedies and by this demonstra-
tion way team achieved to invite people inside [16]. Inspired by this idea,
Karimjee [16] proposes an on-site cast production with Mycobricks and a
continuous factory that supports this biodegradable infrastructure technology
to build a park to host music festivals. The design aims to clean and heal the
land by growing fungi on the polluted soil while using it to produce biode-
gradable mycelium bricks to support t

he desired construction. However, the
proposal focuses on design then production. More recent architectural projects include Rahman, Arredia, and Yassin’s
Kerala pavilion. The mycelium and substrate mixture is layered on a
polygon-shaped wooden structure and does not fully colonize it or serve any
structural function. Dirk E. Hebel and Philippe Block’s Seoul Biennale 2017
Mycotree installation use custom-shaped mycelium bricks of more complex
geometries. Each module is strengthened by wooden joinery to form a self-
supporting tree-shaped structure, utilizing some of the mycelium’s structural
potential [14]. Mayoral explains the term biocomposite as a composite material made of
organic components showing properties better than when each member per-
forms alone [66]. In this manner, he points out the potentials of program-
ming living matter to achieve biocomposites that offer special utilities, such
as the genetic modification or use of bioluminescence. His proposals aim to
achieve a grown, cultivated habitat designed by parametric 3D CAD model-
ing from units made of Greensulate [73]. To accomplish a parametric design,
Mayoral used the 3D Design program Rhinoceros with Grasshopper to create
a modular design with several algorithms to create a unity between the mod-
ules to respond to an urban needs pavilion. He claims that it is possible to
produce fully disposable, biodegradable, and compostable products and
designs ranging from inhabitable structures to insulation by using such a
method. His design proposals are mainly focused on the esthetic properties
and structural potentials of mycelium biocomposites rather than material
properties and their effects on the final product. One of his suggestions pre-
sents a modular 3D structure as a branching pavilion, while another one is a
prototype wall unit to design an inhabitable growing dwelling. Many artists also used a kit from Ecovative to create sculptures or lamps. The Living, an Autodesk funded lab, used this kit to create mycelium bricks
by growing mycelium in molds and baking it once grown [74]. The Fungal
Futures exhibition in 2016 showcased the potential of integrating design
thinking with scientific expertise, resulting in some innovative aspects of
using mycelium at the intersection of design and science [75]. The exhibi-

with mushrooms as a bio-computer to manage growth without a mold while
controlling the conditions over the whole structure by examining and manip-

using an electrode to observe the information transfer over the mycelium as
an electrical output [76]. Using these results as a foundation, Adamatzky
et al. [4] propose their concept design at the cross-section of biofabrication,
functionalizing, and computing a living substrate. Adamatzky et al. [4] proposed a conceptual design for a monolithic living
fungus-based building assembled by a living fungal mycelium enhanced by
nanoparticles and polymers to create a fungal computer. They envision a fun-
gal building would self-grow into desired geometries as designed by the
architect while presenting properties, such as self-repair and waste manage-
ment. It would also be an environmentally adaptive structure that self-
responds to the stimulations as part of a “biological” intelligent building
paradigm. The group outlines the potentials of biofabrication to control the
growth and branching scheme of the mycelium network with a range of
chemical and physical stimuli. Stimulation with light and volatiles is another
potential method for this construction concept. Computing and functionaliz-
ing the electrical current over the mycelium network is the crucial element
for these processes. To examine the outlying potential of mycelium to create such a structure, as
discussed above. Adamatzky et al. [4] observed the electrical current through
the fungus cells as a response to thermal and chemical stimulation by a high-
resolution data logger to test the electrical properties of mycelium networks to
serve as analog computers. Meanwhile, this work demonstrates the potentials of
operating mycelium networks as bio-computers that maintain themselves. The
experiments show that electrical activity recorded on fruit bodies might be a
reliable indicator of the fungi’s reactions to thermal and chemical stimulation. These fruit bodies react like input ports of the fungal computers [4]. Designing and building with mycelium-based composites can create
exciting work in interdisciplinary research with engineering, architecture,
computational design, and biodesign. Yet, Attias et al. [14] claim that many
of the unique qualities and potential implementations of mycelium seem to
be under the radar of designers, which makes it a potent research topic both
in material and design. However, to achieve such inspiring projects under-
standing the mechanics that drives and maintains life for these organisms is
essential. As mycelium-based composites are a relatively new research topic,

17.4
Work of Biodesign Team Turkey on mycelium
composites
TBT, established with an official “Interdisciplinary Collaboration
Protocol” in May 2016, is a research and project development platform. One
of the most important qualities of TBT, which mainly carries out studies on
biodesign, is that it is based on interdisciplinary relationships in its activities
by bringing together individuals from different disciplines, such as bioengi-
neering, architecture, design, civil engineering, chemical engineering, mechan-
ical engineering, and electronic engineering. As Turkey’s first official
biodesign team, TBT has a leading position in the country. The team continues
to work in an academic environment; carries out different initiatives, projects,
and training about biodesign, architecture, and engineering disciplines; and
also aims to contribute to the discovery of organic, ecological, and sustainable
processes in the building sector in cooperation with natural organisms [77]. The issues on which TBT is actively working are the bacterial biocalcifi-
cation process, which aims to create design products by directing the sand
hardening process of a special type of bacteria, the development of innova-
tive urban design models through finding road networks with slime molds,
and the investigation of using potential of mycelium in buildings and design. Accordingly, an ongoing project by the team about mycelium aimed to form
mycelium composites on different substrates with P. ostreatus. Composites
were obtained by a two staged production process which took place in digi-
tally designed molds. TBT examines the effects of different substrates and
fabric reinforcement combinations on structural performance. Mycelium biocomposites in architecture Chapter | 17

17.4.1
Pleurotus ostreatus, its purification, and preparation of
inoculum
Initially, the researchers decided on the fungus type. The choice of mush-
room type had been made following a literature survey. There were about
nine fungal species mentioned in the literature up to 2018. G. lucidum and
P. ostreatus were foun

d to be the most common of these [14]. Besides, pre-
inoculated P. ostreatus mushroom growing kits for hobby growers are easy
to find from both online and local mushroom kit suppliers as a commercial-
ized farming and hobby product. P. ostreatus was used in all experiments (Fig. 17.4). Several growth kits
were ordered for the first stage of the experiments. However, the first set of
packs was contaminated, which could be visually detected even before open-
ing the sealed bags; hence, the strain’s purification became a necessity before
processing further. For the new set of experiments, fungus grown on wheat
was supplied from 1 kg bags from Aras Misel [78], a local company. The purification of the culture was performed by inoculating the petri
dishes containing Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA) before the mushroom was
taken to the experiments. For this, 39 g/L PDA was mixed with distilled
water and then sterilized at 121C for 15 min. After cooling to about 55C,
1315 mL of PDA was transferred to the petri dishes and allowed to solidify
by cooling. Then, the mushroom kit was opened in a laminar airflow cabinet
under aseptic conditions, and 35 wheat grains wrapped with mycelium
were placed on a PDA and incubated for 710 days at 27C. Although it
was better than the kits previously taken at the end of the incubation, several
bacteria and different fungi strains were observed. At this stage, the white
mycelium fragment (B2 mm) of P. ostreatus, which has no other microbial
species nearby, was sub-cultured under aseptic conditions to another PDA
medium. This process has been repeated until there was no other growing

Substrates
Five types of substrates, including wheat straw, cardboard waste, beech

Composite production
The general structure of the growth process found in the literature that details
the production phase is similar to the guide that Ecovative shares on its website
with the public. The process begins with sterilization of the substrate and incu-
bation of this substrate with fungi’s desired type. After letting these fungi colo-
nize a few days in the dark, fabrication breaks the colonized substrate and fills
it into the molds to grow into the desired shape. The process is mostly com-
pleted with drying to evaporate the water to deactivate the living fungus cells. The growth conditions and humidity levels in the literature differ. Substrates in room conditions contain between 5 and 10% moisture, so the
substrates’ moisture rates were calculated for controlled experimentation. Distilled water was used to provide moisture and set the absolute humidity
of the substrates to 75%. Also, 5% by weight of wheat bran was added to the

substrates, following the supplier’s recommendation. Substrates with distilled
water and wheat bran added were sterilized, and then P. ostreatus pellets
were inoculated and incubated for 12 days in a dark environment at 27C. The plastic bags’ ventilation was provided by placing plug-shaped cotton in
the mouth of the bag to prevent contamination. After the first incubation period, the substrates were removed from plastic
bags and transferred to molds; the mold surfaces were pre-sterilized with alco-
hol. In the molds, ventilation was provided via appropriately sized holes, and
the molds were placed in large bags to prevent contamination. Then, incuba-
tion was continued for another 18 days at the same temperature. At the end of
the period, the composite materials were removed from the molds and dried
first at 120C for 90 min to eliminate further fungal cell production and 50C
for 48 more hours to dry out completely (33% average final weight). Mycelial
development and the integrity of the composites were evaluated. Both grain transfer and pellet inoculation methods were determined to
obtain sufficient inoculum and tested on substrates. However, homogeneous
mycelium growth could not be achieved on each grain of wheat. Therefore
when the results were evaluated, it was decided to use pellets for future pro-
duction to ensure the inoculum distribution standardization. Incubation time is one of the essential decisions that should be made
according to the mushroom type sin

ce speeding up the process is necessary for
the industrialization of mycelium-based products. Therefore further research
over the coherence of different substrates and fungi types is essential. Attias
et al. [14] suggest that short incubation periods would restrict organic matter
digestion; thus the wood fibers in the substrate preserve their structural proper-
ties. On the other hand, keeping the incubation period longer would cause
more digestion and lignocellulose content changes—the processing time and
product quality are correlated. Longer colonization time is required to achieve
a stiffer and stronger final product. A smoother skin-like surface can also be
achieved through full colonization [38]. According to this information, tests
run over different growth periods with selected combinations of substrate and
fungi must be analyzed to determine the optimal structural properties achieved
by particular levels of substrate and subsequent mycelium formation. Depending on the substrate or fungus type, these parameters may change. Substrate type is important here from two aspects. First, the substrate sup-
ports the relevant organism’s growth by serving as both a food source and a
scaffold. The organism will hold the material together by spreading between
the substrate particles, showing better mycelial growth when the proper
nutrient medium (suitable substrate) is provided. Visual observation shows
faster and denser colonization over cardboard waste, cotton textile waste,
and wheat straw cultivation jars, which performed better. The colonization
speed of mycelium over cotton textile waste showed an eye-catching perfor-
mance. It is possibly because of the buckled formation of cotton textile that
resulted in creating air gaps within the substrate’s general structure, allowing
508

more freedom for organisms to form while allowing better air circulation. However, textile waste is hard to cut into smaller pieces and requires extra
machinery to enable processing. Also, as a recyclable material, textile waste
has more uses and hence value than the other candidates. Thus according to
preliminary visual observations and analysis, focusing on wheat straw and
cardboard waste were chosen as suitable substrates for ongoing experiments. The second significant factor for a substrate is the particle size. If the par-
ticle size of the substrate is too large, the mycelium cannot hold the particles
homogeneously and strongly together. If the particle size of the substrate is
too small, there will be no gaps between them. There will not be enough
space for the organism to grow, and there are no air gaps for the aerobic
organism, which will result in a non-homogenous distribution of organic
mass, as in Fig. 17.9. The type of substrate, in this case, is a handy parame-
ter. Not micron size, but small particles visible to the naked eye are observed
to be the most effective size. However, a g

eneralization could not be made
because of different substrates’ changing properties like cotton or fabric
waste. Also, the substrate’s properties will affect the final properties of the
composite, as the whole substrate is not digested. Therefore the substrate
type will affect the composite’s stiffness, strength, thermal, and acoustic
properties. Research shows that substrates in a ground form are better than
using these substrates as dust or larger pieces to cultivate the mushroom. During the first observations, the capacity of the moisture held by the sub-
strate changes with its chemical properties, size, and shape. While it was
observed that small particles obtained with an industrial grinder colonize fas-
ter in terms of mycelial development compared to larger particles, smaller
particles were found to be more homogenous, and the obtained material had
better integrity. After designing and crafting the negative mold that meets fungi’s needs
for growth (air circulations, contamination protection, moisture holding,
etc.), the only energy-consuming needs are keeping the mold in the state of
optimal heat and moisture condition. However, it will not be difficult to
maintain these conditions in many cases since the organism tends to grow
optimally near room temperature. And for the scenarios responding to differ-
ent regions of the world, another type of fungus can be chosen accordingly

17.5.1.1
Test setup: Substrate and sterilization
Professional clean room conditions in addition to the necessity of using
gloves and a sterilized container with a HEPA-filter was needed. A relatively
sterile environment has been reached, through the application of a gas cooker
to sterilize all surfaces that are in contact with the colonizing substrate and
disinfecting them with alcohol ( . 70%) to avoid disinfection or contamina-
tion risks. To sterilize the substrate, an autoclave or pressure cooker was
required, where it was sterilized for over a period of 6090 min at tempera-
tures above 121C that allowed the majority of all germs to be killed. The
risk of contamination can also be reduced through cooking at 100C that
should considerably reduce the risk of germination by about 90%. After testing with different substrates, the only ingredients that were used were
grains of cereals that functioned in the pretests. These had the advantage of being
rich in protein and nutrients, which allows the mycelium to rapidly incubate. For the sterilization of the substrate, an autoclavable polypropylene con-
tainer with a capacity of 0.6 L was used. Alternatively, preserving jars, with
piercing holes and filter wool, were also used. FIGURE 17.12

The production process of the material from

1. Soak the substrate for a few minutes in hot water and then press the

are put into the molds layer by layer). 5. The mushroom must have a humid environment either by controlling the
room humidity or by additional packing in holes provided with needle-

by daily or regular spraying to maintain moisture, but the mycelium can
be attached to the mold surface. 6. Initiating growth through storage under warm and humid conditions,
while disinfecting the molds with an alcohol solution. 7. Growth is stopped, and the hardening process is achieved through heating
the material at 50C60C. To avoid deformation in this drying process,
applying extra pressure on the mold may be needed. 17.5.1.4

17.6
Conclusion
This chapter presents two separate but complementary academic stud-
ies from different departments related to the properties and potential uses of
mycelium-based biocomposites produced by P. ostreatus in buildings. Both
studies focused on the colonization properties of P. ostreatus over several
types of substrates as a function of their lignin content. Findings indicate
that the substrate’s size and lignin proportion, molding strategies, and envi-
ronmental conditions are beneficial throughout the colonization phase. This
processing approach can be used to directly fabricate finished product(s) dur-
ing this first colonization phase; on the other hand, the method explained in
the third section would be an efficient alternative for the production of larger
elements and on-site production for design applications. This research out-
lines the importance of optimizing three different aspects of such a produc-
tion process: growth method and conditions, choice of substrate type, and
the interrelationship between the molding step, the design, and quality of the
final product. The first stage of the mycelium material production heavily depends
on sterilization and a controllable production environment with conditions
like temperature and humidity under constant conditions. This brings the
requirements for a specific mold design, at least for the first colonization
process. Skin layers that increase the air circulation in aseptic conditions are
observed to result in faster colonization. Furthermore, to strengthen the final
product, fabric reinforcement has been examined by both of the studies
and observed to increase the integrity of the composite drastically. Since the

Illustration of the application scenarios of the analyzed developments in differ-
ent architectural contexts. Source: L. Strobel and BioMat at ITKE/University of Stuttgart. Mycelium biocomposites in architecture Chapter | 17
521

textile’s main fabric is the unity of the natural woven fibers, the fibers
of hyphae would be efficient to bring out a form aligned with these fibers
and even merge to strengthen the connections within the elements of the
composite. This chapter indicates that by controlling the environment and the
organism’s colonization process, it is possible to use mycelium with fabric
reinforcement to be an in situ construction approach for more sustainable
architecture and buildings in the future. However, there are questions con-
cerning the stability of the final products and long-term endurance when
confronted by challenging environmental conditions; this is continues to be
a significant issue for outdoor application

s. Therefore future research inves-
tigating rugged coatings and deposition technologies would be essential for
applications of these novel materials to become widespread throughout the
construction industry. Acknowledgments
